{{Short description|Number of occurrences of a repeating event per unit of time}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Frequency
| image = FrequencyAnimation.gif
| caption = A sinusoidal wave: higher frequency means more cycles per unit time.
| topics = [[Wave]], [[Oscillation]], [[Simple harmonic motion]], [[Electromagnetic wave]]
| symbol = f, ν
| SI_unit = [[hertz]] (Hz)
| dimension = T⁻¹
}}

'''Frequency''' is the number of occurrences of a repeating event per unit of [[time]]. It is measured in [[hertz]] (Hz), where 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second. Frequency is a fundamental parameter in describing [[wave]]s, [[oscillation]]s, and periodic phenomena across all branches of physics.

:<math>f = \frac{1}{T}</math>
where <math>T</math> is the [[period (physics)|period]] (the duration of one cycle).

== Angular frequency ==
[[Angular frequency]] <math>\omega</math> (in radians per second) is related to ordinary frequency by:
:<math>\omega = 2\pi f</math>

== Wave relationships ==
For a [[wave]] travelling at speed <math>v</math> with [[wavelength]] <math>\lambda</math>, the frequency is:
:<math>f = \frac{v}{\lambda}</math>

For [[electromagnetic wave]]s in vacuum (<math>v = c</math>):
:<math>c = \lambda f</math>

For [[sound wave]]s, frequency determines [[pitch (music)|pitch]].

== Frequency ranges ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Range !! Frequency (Hz)
|-
| [[Radio wave]]s || ~3 kHz – 300 GHz
|-
| [[Microwave]]s || ~300 MHz – 300 GHz
|-
| [[Infrared]] || ~300 GHz – 430 THz
|-
| [[Visible light]] || ~430–790 THz
|-
| [[Ultraviolet]] || ~790 THz – 30 PHz
|-
| [[X-ray]]s || ~30 PHz – 30 EHz
|-
| [[Gamma ray]]s || > 30 EHz
|}

== Quantum relation ==
In [[quantum mechanics]], frequency is related to [[energy]] by the [[Planck–Einstein relation]]:
:<math>E = hf = \hbar\omega</math>
where <math>h</math> is the [[Planck constant]]. This is fundamental to the [[photoelectric effect]], [[spectroscopy]], and all quantum processes.

== Resonance ==
{{Main|Resonance}}
When a system is driven at its [[natural frequency]], the amplitude of oscillation increases dramatically—a phenomenon called [[resonance]].

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Angular frequency]]
* [[Electromagnetic spectrum]]
* [[Hertz]]
* [[Oscillation]]
* [[Wavelength]]
* [[Wave]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Frequency| ]]
[[Category:Wave mechanics]]
[[Category:Temporal rates]]
[[Category:Acoustics]]
